Title: Boron Toxicity Modulates Hypocotyl Growth Through Brassinosteroid and Thermomorphogenic‐Like Mechanisms

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Leucine-rich repeat receptor-like kinase; brassinosteroid receptor
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Promotes hypocotyl cell elongation and seedling growth under boron toxicity
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Reduced BRI1 function attenuates boron-toxicity-induced hypocotyl elongation, indicating BR perception is required for the growth response
Common_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Boron toxicity (3 mM H3BO3); exogenous epiBL; brassinazole; warm temperature
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Loss-of-function mutant phenotyping (bri1-101); hypocotyl length measurements under control and boron toxicity; pharmacological comparison with brassinazole and epiBL
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. bri1-101 seedlings had shorter hypocotyls than wild type under both control and boron-toxicity conditions. 2. The reduction was more pronounced under boron toxicity, and the mutation began to interfere with the elongation response by 5 DPG. 3. These data support that BR perception through BRI1 is necessary for full boron-toxicity-induced hypocotyl elongation.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced growth due to impaired BR sensitivity
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Although BRI1 is central to BR signalling, the paper primarily uses it as a signaling component rather than a transcriptional regulator or biosynthetic enzyme.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Manipulating BR perception could tune boron-toxicity growth responses, but reduced BRI1 activity causes strong growth penalties and is unlikely to be beneficial alone.

Common_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: GSK3/Shaggy-like protein kinase; negative regulator of BR signaling
Common_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Promotes hypocotyl elongation through active BR signaling under boron toxicity
Common_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Inactivation of BIN2/BIL1/BIL2 enhances sensitivity to boron toxicity and increases hypocotyl elongation, supporting a repressive role of BIN2 on the BR-dependent growth response
Common_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Reverse genetics using bin2-bil1bil2 constitutive-signaling mutant; hypocotyl phenotyping under control and boron toxicity
Common_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. The bin2-bil1bil2 mutant displayed greater boron-toxicity sensitivity than wild type at 5 and 7 DPG. 2. Relative hypocotyl elongation under boron toxicity was significantly greater in the mutant. 3. These data indicate repression of BIN2-family kinases potentiates the BR-mediated elongation response.
Common_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Constitutive BR signaling may cause pleiotropic growth alterations
Common_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper discusses the triple mutant as constitutively active BR signalling due to inactivation of BIN2, BIL1, and BIL2 repressors.
Common_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Partial attenuation of BIN2-family repression might enhance growth under boron stress, but constitutive BR activation likely has pleiotropic costs.

Pathway_Genes.Metabolic_Step_Position: Upstream to midstream; rate-controlling biosynthetic step
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates multiple brassinosteroid precursors at C-22 during bioactive BR biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Supplies precursor pool for castasterone and brassinolide biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Promotes cell elongation and hypocotyl growth
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: BT upregulates DWF4 expression, supporting increased BR biosynthesis associated with sustained castasterone accumulation and hypocotyl elongation
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Upregulated under boron toxicity from 3 to 7 DPG, reduced at 10 DPG
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Hypocotyl under boron toxicity from 3 to 7 DPG
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Boron toxicity (3 mM H3BO3); brassinazole treatment discussed as inhibitor of DWF4 activity
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RT-qPCR of hypocotyl tissue across development; hormone profiling of castasterone; pharmacological inhibition with brassinazole
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. DWF4 transcript abundance increased under boron toxicity from 3 to 7 DPG. 2. This upregulation correlated with elevated castasterone accumulation and enhanced hypocotyl elongation. 3. Together with brassinazole sensitivity, the data support increased DWF4-dependent BR biosynthesis under boron toxicity.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Enhanced BR biosynthesis may broadly alter growth programs
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper explicitly describes DWF4 as a rate-limiting BR biosynthetic gene and notes downregulation at 10 DPG despite sustained high CS, likely due to reduced inactivation.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A promising target to modulate BR supply under boron stress, but tuning rather than constitutive overactivation would be advisable due to pleiotropic BR effects.

Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: C-6 oxidation steps toward bioactive BRs
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Castasterone and related downstream oxidized BR intermediates
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes C-6 oxidation reactions in the late brassinosteroid biosynthetic pathway contributing to castasterone/brassinolide formation
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Forms immediate downstream bioactive BR pool
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Promotes hypocotyl cell expansion and elongation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: BT transiently elevates BR6OX1 expression, supporting enhanced BR biosynthesis linked to castasterone accumulation and hypocotyl elongation
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Upregulated under boron toxicity from 3 to 5 DPG; returned to basal by 7 DPG; reduced at 10 DPG
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Hypocotyl under boron toxicity, strongest at 3-5 DPG
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RT-qPCR in hypocotyls; correlation with castasterone measurements
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BR6OX1 expression increased under boron toxicity early in seedling development. 2. Its induction paralleled increased castasterone accumulation and enhanced hypocotyl growth. 3. The effect was transient relative to BR6OX2, suggesting stage-specific contribution to BR production.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Broad BR-related pleiotropic growth effects possible
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The discussion groups BR6OX1 with DWF4 and BR6OX2 as key/rate-limiting BR biosynthetic enzymes.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Could be combined with other BR-pathway genes for stress-responsive growth tuning; stage-specific control may be preferable.

Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Castasterone and related late BR intermediates
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Brassinolide; contributes to bioactive BR pool
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Catalyzes late C-6 oxidation reactions including conversion steps linked to castasterone/brassinolide biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Directly forms terminal bioactive BR or its immediate precursor
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: BT strongly upregulates BR6OX2, consistent with enhanced terminal BR biosynthesis and prolonged hypocotyl elongation under boron toxicity
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Upregulated from 3 to 7 DPG and remained elevated until 10 DPG under boron toxicity
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Hypocotyl under boron toxicity across most developmental stages tested
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RT-qPCR of hypocotyl tissue; castasterone quantification; pharmacological BR perturbation
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BR6OX2 showed prolonged induction under boron toxicity, lasting longer than BR6OX1 or DWF4. 2. This pattern is consistent with sustained bioactive BR production during later developmental stages. 3. The authors propose BR6OX2 may support continued BR-driven hypocotyl elongation under boron toxicity.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Elevated BR pathway output may affect whole-plant architecture
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The paper emphasizes BR6OX2 as important for CS-to-BL conversion, although BL itself was not detected.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A strong candidate for engineering sustained BR output under boron stress, especially with inducible or tissue-specific expression strategies.

Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Key control point for BR homeostasis
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Hydroxylates bioactive brassinosteroids such as castasterone and brassinolide at C-26, reducing their activity
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: Competes with target-product accumulation by inactivating bioactive BRs
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Active BRs promote hypocotyl elongation; BAS1 limits this by hormone inactivation
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: BT generally represses BAS1 expression, reducing BR inactivation and thereby supporting sustained bioactive BR accumulation and hypocotyl elongation
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Generally downregulated under boron toxicity, except a transient increase at 5 DPG
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Hypocotyl under boron toxicity; mostly reduced across development
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RT-qPCR in hypocotyls correlated with castasterone quantification
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BAS1 expression was broadly suppressed by boron toxicity. 2. Reduced BAS1 expression is consistent with decreased BR inactivation and sustained castasterone accumulation. 3. A transient increase at 5 DPG may reflect a localized homeostatic attempt to buffer BR excess.
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reducing BR inactivation may exaggerate BR-mediated pleiotropic growth effects
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The authors interpret BAS1 repression as evidence that boron toxicity disrupts normal BR negative feedback homeostasis.
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Down-tuning BAS1 could enhance BR-mediated growth under boron stress, but strong suppression may destabilize hormone homeostasis.

Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Transcriptional activation in BR signaling; activity controlled by phosphorylation status
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Brassinosteroid signaling and promotion of cell elongation; likely positive control of BR biosynthesis feedback under boron toxicity
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Cell elongation-related genes; possibly PIF4-associated targets; BR biosynthesis genes in feedback context
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Promotes BR-responsive growth programs; under BT its increased expression is associated with enhanced signaling
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Brassinosteroids; BRI1-mediated signaling; BIN2 repression; boron toxicity
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Paper proposes BT disrupts normal negative feedback and may convert BR homeostasis into a positive feedback mechanism
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Likely functions with PIF4; discussed as BZR1-PIF4 heterodimer based on prior literature
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Key positive BR signaling regulator supporting sustained hypocotyl elongation under boron toxicity
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Promotes hypocotyl cell expansion and growth
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: BZR1 expression is induced at later stages under boron toxicity, supporting enhanced BR signaling associated with sustained hypocotyl elongation
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Repressed at 3 DPG, upregulated at 5 and 7 DPG, no difference at 10 DPG under boron toxicity
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Hypocotyl; stage-dependent induction under boron toxicity
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Boron toxicity (3 mM H3BO3); BR-dependent signaling context
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RT-qPCR; genetic/pharmacological perturbation of BR signaling pathway; hypocotyl phenotyping
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BZR1 transcripts were transiently reduced early but increased at later developmental stages under boron toxicity. 2. The expression pattern supports enhanced BR signaling during sustained hypocotyl elongation. 3. The authors propose BZR1 may help drive PIF4 upregulation and growth under boron toxicity.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Enhanced BR signaling may trigger broad developmental pleiotropy
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Direct mutant validation for BZR1 was not performed in this paper; evidence is transcript-level plus pathway genetics/pharmacology.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A useful regulatory node for tuning BR-driven elongation responses, but should be manipulated carefully due to strong pleiotropic effects.

Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Transcriptional activation in BR signaling; activity controlled by phosphorylation/dephosphorylation
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Brassinosteroid signaling and elongation-associated gene expression
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Cell elongation-related genes; likely BR biosynthetic genes together with PIF4 in proposed model
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Promotes BR-responsive gene expression under active signaling
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Brassinosteroids; BRI1 signaling; BIN2 repression; boron toxicity
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Discussed as part of disrupted BR feedback under boron toxicity and possible positive feedback with PIF4
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: Likely BES1-PIF4 complex proposed from prior literature and the authors' model
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Key BR signaling effector whose increased expression under boron toxicity supports prolonged elongation response
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Promotes hypocotyl elongation and cell expansion
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: BES1 is upregulated from 5 to 10 DPG under boron toxicity, consistent with sustained BR signaling and continued hypocotyl elongation
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Repressed at 3 DPG; upregulated from 5 to 10 DPG under boron toxicity
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Hypocotyl under boron toxicity at later stages
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: RT-qPCR; integration with BR signaling mutant and pharmacological data
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. BES1 transcript levels were reduced early and increased strongly at later stages under boron toxicity. 2. This pattern is consistent with prolonged BR signaling during sustained hypocotyl elongation. 3. The authors propose BES1 may partner with PIF4 to enhance BR biosynthesis gene expression under boron toxicity.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Strong BES1 activation may cause pleiotropic growth effects
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: Evidence is indirect within this paper; no BES1 mutant was tested here.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Potential leverage point for engineering BR-responsive growth under boron stress, ideally through conditional regulation.

Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: bHLH transcription factor; phytochrome-interacting factor family
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Transcriptional activation of elongation programs; thermomorphogenesis regulator
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Growth regulatory integration of BR signaling and thermomorphogenic-like hypocotyl elongation
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Cell elongation-related genes; BR biosynthesis genes in proposed feedback/feedforward interaction
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Promotes expression of elongation-related genes and may enhance BR biosynthesis gene expression in conjunction with BES1/BZR1
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Boron toxicity; warm temperature-like signaling; COP1; BR signaling; PHYB/ELF3 repression context
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Proposed feedforward interaction with BR signaling and BR biosynthetic gene activation under boron toxicity
Regulation_Genes.Protein_Complex_Involvement: PIF4-BZR1 heterodimer; PIF4-BES1 complex proposed in model/discussion
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Essential positive regulator of boron-toxicity-induced hypocotyl elongation sharing mechanism with thermomorphogenesis
Regulation_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Supports hypocotyl elongation under boron toxicity
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss of PIF4 function reduces boron-toxicity-induced hypocotyl elongation, especially at later developmental stages
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Reduced at 3 DPG, increased from 5 to 7 DPG, no significant difference at 10 DPG under boron toxicity
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Hypocotyl under boron toxicity; stage-dependent induction
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Boron toxicity (3 mM H3BO3); warm temperature comparison
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Loss-of-function mutant analysis (pif4, pif4pif5, pifq); hypocotyl phenotyping; RT-qPCR
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. pif mutants, especially pif4, showed reduced hypocotyl elongation under boron toxicity. 2. PIF4 transcripts increased at later developmental stages under boron toxicity. 3. The data support PIF4 as a major positive regulator linking boron toxicity responses to thermomorphogenic-like growth and BR signaling.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced PIF4 activity suppresses growth; overactivation could cause excessive elongation
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: PIF4 is one of the clearest core regulators experimentally validated in the Results section.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: A strong target to modulate stress-adaptive elongation, but deployment should balance beneficial elongation versus unwanted shade/heat-growth traits.

Regulation_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: RING-finger E3 ubiquitin ligase; photomorphogenesis regulator
Regulation_Genes.Regulation_Mode: Protein degradation control and signaling promotion; indirectly supports elongation regulators
Regulation_Genes.Metabolic_Process_Controlled: Thermomorphogenic-like signaling and BR-linked hypocotyl elongation under boron toxicity
Regulation_Genes.Primary_Regulatory_Targets: Photomorphogenesis repressors such as HY5; indirectly supports PIF4 and BR signaling outputs
Regulation_Genes.Regulatory_Effect_on_Target_Genes: Indirectly promotes elongation-associated programs by repressing photomorphogenesis factors
Regulation_Genes.Upstream_Signals_or_Inputs: Boron toxicity; light signaling; BR signaling context
Regulation_Genes.Feedback_or_Feedforward_Regulation: Potential bidirectional reinforcement with BR signaling discussed in model
Regulation_Genes.Decisive_Influence_on_Target_Product: Essential positive regulator for full boron-toxicity-induced hypocotyl elongation
Regulation_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Loss-of-function cop1 mutants show reduced or suppressed boron-toxicity-induced hypocotyl elongation, indicating COP1 is required for the response
Regulation_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Increased under boron toxicity at most stages, reduced at 10 DPG
Regulation_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Boron toxicity (3 mM H3BO3); warm temperature-like mechanism comparison
Regulation_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Loss-of-function mutant analysis (cop1-4, cop1-6); hypocotyl phenotyping; RT-qPCR
Regulation_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. cop1 mutants had reduced hypocotyl elongation under boron toxicity, with cop1-6 showing the strongest suppression. 2. COP1 transcript levels were elevated under boron toxicity at most stages. 3. These results support COP1 as a major positive regulator linking boron toxicity to thermomorphogenic-like and BR-associated elongation mechanisms.
Regulation_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced COP1 function impairs elongation; altered COP1 activity likely affects broader light-development pathways
Regulation_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The authors propose BT may enhance COP1 activity and possibly reinforce BR signaling by effects on BIN2/BZR1/BES1.
Regulation_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Possible target to tune stress-growth signaling, but high pleiotropy in light development makes precise modulation essential.
